name: Reflexion (Shinn et al., 2023) / Self-Refine (Madaan et al., 2023) — Self-Evaluation and Improvement Loops for LLMs
origin_domain: AI research (large language model agents, self-improvement, reinforcement learning)
year_developed: Both 2023. Reflexion: arXiv:2303.11366, submitted March 20, 2023; published at NeurIPS 2023. Self-Refine: arXiv:2303.17651, March 30, 2023; published at NeurIPS 2023 / ICLR 2024. These two papers were developed concurrently and independently, together establishing the self-evaluation and self-improvement paradigm for LLM agents.
progenitor: Reflexion: Noah Shinn, Federico Cassano, Edward Berman, Ashwin Gopinath, Karthik Narasimhan, Shunyu Yao (Northeastern University, Princeton University, MIT). Shunyu Yao is also the first author of ReAct and Tree of Thoughts. Self-Refine: Aman Madaan, Niket Tandon, Prakhar Gupta, Skyler Hallinan, Luyu Gao, Sarah Wiegreffe, Uri Alon, Nouha Dziri, Shrimai Prabhumoye, Yiming Yang, Shashank Gupta, Bodhisattwa Prasad Majumder, Katherine Hermann, Sean Welleck, Amir Yazdanbakhsh, Peter Clark (Carnegie Mellon University, Allen Institute for AI, University of Washington, and other institutions). Peter Clark (Allen AI) is a senior author and prominent figure in NLP reasoning research.
driving_insight: LLMs, when prompted appropriately, can critique their own outputs with useful specificity — they can identify what went wrong and suggest how to fix it. This capability can be harnessed to create self-improvement loops that operate entirely at inference time, without fine-tuning, without external reward models, and without human feedback. The key insight has two variants. Reflexion's insight: frame the self-improvement loop as 'verbal reinforcement learning' — the agent acts, observes the outcome, verbally reflects on what went wrong and why, stores that reflection in episodic memory, and uses it to guide future attempts. This converts scalar or textual feedback signals into actionable linguistic insights that improve subsequent trials. Self-Refine's insight: the same LLM can play all three roles — GENERATOR (produces initial output), FEEDBACK provider (critiques its own output against task-specific criteria), and REFINER (revises the output based on feedback) — in an iterative loop. This mirrors how humans improve their own work: draft, self-critique, revise, repeat. The shared finding is that LLMs possess latent self-critique and self-improvement capabilities that can be unlocked purely through prompting, producing 20-91% improvement across diverse tasks without any training.
decision_style: deliberative and iterative — the model explicitly deliberates about what went wrong and how to improve, then decides on a revised approach. The decision process is: (1) the model generates an initial attempt (first decision); (2) the model evaluates the outcome (second deliberation — what was good/bad?); (3) the model decides how to improve (third deliberation — what should change?); (4) the model generates a revised attempt. This is a form of meta-cognition: the model reasons about its own reasoning and decisions, not just about the task. The decision style is self-reflective — the model is both the decision-maker and the evaluator of its own decisions. In Reflexion, the reflection is stored in episodic memory, creating a growing body of 'lessons learned' that inform future decisions. In Self-Refine, the reflection is used immediately to refine the current output. Both approaches are deliberative (explicit reasoning about quality) rather than algorithmic (the improvement strategy is not a pre-programmed algorithm but emerges from the LLM's reasoning).
problem_class_targeted: Tasks where: (a) output quality can be meaningfully evaluated (by the LLM itself, by an external signal, or by the environment); (b) iterative improvement is possible (the task admits revision); (c) the gap between initial output quality and acceptable quality is bridgeable through self-critique and revision. Reflexion was demonstrated on: sequential decision-making (ALFWorld — household tasks with action sequences), code generation (HumanEval — writing Python functions), and language reasoning (HotpotQA — multi-hop QA). Self-Refine was demonstrated on: dialogue response generation, code optimization, math reasoning, sentiment reversal, acronym generation, constrained text generation, and text detoxification (7 diverse tasks). Both approaches target tasks where a single-pass generation is often inadequate and iterative refinement improves quality. Less suited to: tasks where evaluation is fundamentally ambiguous (no clear right/wrong, subjective quality), tasks where the initial output is so poor that self-critique cannot guide meaningful improvement, and tasks where the model has no ability to evaluate quality (the model doesn't know what it doesn't know). The self-critique paradox — LLMs can only critique based on what they can recognize as wrong, and they have systematic blind spots — limits applicability.
ordered_phases: Both approaches share a core iterative loop. Reflexion's phases: (1) ACT — the agent takes actions in the environment (generates code, performs search, makes decisions); (2) OBSERVE — the agent receives feedback (execution results, scalar reward, test pass/fail); (3) REFLECT — the agent generates a verbal reflection: what went wrong, why, and what should be done differently next time. This reflection is stored in episodic memory; (4) REPEAT — the agent attempts the task again, informed by the episodic memory of previous reflections. This is an outer loop around a ReAct-style inner loop. Self-Refine's phases: (1) GENERATE — the LLM produces an initial output; (2) FEEDBACK — the same LLM evaluates its output and provides multi-aspect, actionable feedback (what is good, what is lacking, specific suggestions); (3) REFINE — the LLM revises the output based on the feedback; (4) REPEAT — iterate the FEEDBACK and REFINE steps until quality plateaus (typically 1-3 iterations). Self-Refine does not involve an external environment — it is pure linguistic self-improvement. Both approaches have a clear iterative structure but no phase gates or formal phase transitions — the loop continues until quality stops improving or a maximum iteration count is reached.
feedback_loops: Both approaches are built around a central feedback loop. Reflexion's feedback loop: Environment -> Observation -> Reflection -> Episodic Memory -> Next Attempt. The environment provides hard feedback (test pass/fail, task success/failure), which the model converts into linguistic reflection that guides the next attempt. The episodic memory accumulates reflections across attempts, creating a learning feedback loop — later attempts benefit from the accumulated wisdom of earlier failures. Self-Refine's feedback loop: Output -> Self-Feedback -> Refined Output -> Self-Feedback -> ... . This is a tight, purely linguistic feedback loop with no external environment — the LLM generates both the output and the feedback. The feedback quality depends on the LLM's ability to critically evaluate its own work. Both loops are explicit (designed into the framework) and iterative (repeat until convergence). A key difference: Reflexion's feedback includes hard environmental signals (test results, success/failure), which grounds the reflection in reality; Self-Refine's feedback is purely self-generated, which risks self-reinforcement of errors if the model's self-evaluation is flawed.
gates: No formal gates in either approach. Implicit gates: (a) convergence check — the model (or an external metric) determines whether the output has improved enough to stop iterating; (b) maximum iteration limit — the loop stops after N attempts regardless of quality; (c) in Reflexion, the environment provides a hard success/failure gate — if the task succeeds, the loop terminates. What happens when a 'gate' blocks: in Self-Refine, if the feedback indicates no further improvement is needed, the loop stops and the current output is returned. In Reflexion, if the task consistently fails after maximum attempts, the best attempt (or a failure report) is returned. The absence of formal gates means the process can iterate too long (cost overrun) or stop too early (premature convergence). The Self-Refine paper found diminishing returns after 1-2 iterations for most tasks, suggesting that a simple iteration cap (2-3 iterations) is a practical substitute for formal convergence gates.
exit_criteria: Reflexion: terminates when the task succeeds (environment returns success) OR the maximum number of trials is exhausted. The final output is the successful trial's result or the best attempt across all trials. Self-Refine: terminates when the FEEDBACK step indicates the output is satisfactory (no substantive improvements identified) OR the maximum number of refinement iterations is reached (typically 2-3) OR the quality score plateaus (no improvement between iterations). The Self-Refine paper found that most gains occur in the first 1-2 iterations, with minimal improvement thereafter — this provides practical guidance for setting exit criteria. Partial completion: both approaches produce intermediate outputs that may be useful even if the process does not fully converge.
decomposition_method: Neither approach decomposes the task in the traditional sense. Self-Refine decomposes the improvement process (not the task) into: what aspects of the output need improvement, and how to improve each aspect. The FEEDBACK step produces multi-aspect feedback (e.g., 'The code is correct but inefficient — the loop could be replaced with a list comprehension. Also, the variable names could be clearer.'), which decomposes quality issues into actionable items. Reflexion decomposes the learning process by storing individual reflections as separate episodic memories, each addressing a specific failure mode. However, neither approach decomposes the original task into sub-tasks — they are quality improvement loops, not task decomposition methods. For complex tasks, these approaches are typically combined with task decomposition methods (P&E, ReAct, AutoGen) to improve the quality of individual subtask outputs.
granularity_guidance: In Self-Refine, the granularity of refinement is determined by the FEEDBACK prompt: feedback can be general ('improve the overall quality') or detailed ('fix the grammar in paragraph 2, clarify the argument in paragraph 3'). The Self-Refine paper uses detailed, multi-aspect feedback prompts that guide the model to address specific issues. The FEEDBACK step naturally regulates granularity: it identifies specific problems, and the REFINE step addresses them. In Reflexion, the granularity of reflection is the per-trial level: after each complete attempt, the model reflects on what went wrong. The granularity of stored reflections is one reflection per trial, creating a coarse-grained episodic memory. Neither approach provides formal granularity guidance — the granularity emerges from the prompt design and the model's judgment.
boundary_rules: No formal boundary rules. In Self-Refine, the FEEDBACK step separates the evaluator role from the generator role, but both are the same LLM — there is no true role isolation. The model is prompted to 'act as a critic' but has access to its own generation context. This blurs the boundary between generation and evaluation. In Reflexion, the ACT and REFLECT phases are distinct — the agent first acts, then reflects — but again, the same model performs both. The functional separation is achieved through prompting, not architectural isolation. The lack of hard boundaries is a recognized limitation: the model's self-evaluation is inherently biased because it shares the same knowledge, assumptions, and blind spots as the model that generated the output. This is the 'self-critique paradox' — the evaluator cannot catch errors that the generator doesn't know are errors.
risk_assessment_mechanism: No formal risk assessment. Risk is assessed implicitly through: (a) the FEEDBACK step — if the self-critique identifies serious problems, that signals elevated risk; (b) the convergence behavior — if quality does not improve between iterations, the approach may be stuck (risk of wasted iterations); (c) in Reflexion, environment failures are risk signals. The approaches provide no: probabilistic risk scoring, risk classification, or systematic risk management. The self-evaluation mechanism is a QUALITY assessment, not a RISK assessment — it tells you if the output is good, not if it's safe. For high-stakes applications, self-evaluation alone is insufficient for risk management because the model may not recognize dangerous outputs as problematic.
unknown_handling: When the model is uncertain or makes an error: (1) Self-Refine's FEEDBACK step identifies unknown/uncertain aspects and suggests specific improvements (e.g., 'The explanation of X is unclear — it should define Y more precisely'). (2) Reflexion's REFLECT step generalizes from specific failures to broader lessons (e.g., 'When the search results are contradictory, I should verify with additional sources rather than choosing the first result'). This converts specific unknowns into general strategies for handling similar unknowns in future attempts. However, both approaches are limited by the model's ability to recognize unknowns — the model cannot critique what it doesn't know it doesn't know. If the model is confidently wrong (it believes a false statement is true), it will not identify this as a problem in self-evaluation. This is the fundamental limitation of self-improvement without external ground truth.
default_stance_on_unknown: PERMISSIVE — the model generates despite uncertainty, and self-evaluation identifies and corrects issues post-hoc. This is the core operating model: act first, evaluate after, improve iteratively. The permissive stance is moderated by: (a) in Reflexion, the environment provides hard feedback that constrains erroneous actions; (b) in Self-Refine, the FEEDBACK step prevents obviously flawed outputs from being considered final; (c) the iterative loop ensures that if the first attempt is poor due to unknowns, subsequent iterations have the opportunity to improve. The permissive default is appropriate for low-stakes generation tasks (text, code) where errors in early iterations are non-catastrophic. For high-stakes tasks, additional guardrails (external verification, human review) are needed because self-evaluation is an unreliable gate for safety-critical errors.
safety_guardrails: Minimal built-in safety mechanisms. The primary safety features are: (a) in Reflexion, the environment bounds the action space — the agent can only take predefined actions, limiting the scope of potential harm; (b) in Self-Refine, the process is purely linguistic — no external actions are taken, so the worst-case outcome is a low-quality text output; (c) the iterative loop provides multiple opportunities to catch and correct problems. Both approaches share standard LLM vulnerabilities: (a) the model may not recognize harmful content as problematic (if it was trained on data that normalizes harm); (b) in Reflexion, the agent can take harmful actions in the environment if the action space includes dangerous functions; (c) the reflection memory in Reflexion could accumulate harmful heuristics ('When asked to do X, always do Y' where Y is unsafe) that persist across attempts. For production use, these approaches must be combined with external safety filters, action sandboxing, and human review for high-stakes outputs.
human_decision_points: Neither approach has built-in human-in-the-loop. Both are designed as fully autonomous self-improvement loops. Humans can be inserted at: (a) final output review — the human evaluates the converged output before accepting it; (b) reflection/feedback review — a human reviews the model's self-critique and can add or correct it; (c) iteration gating — the human decides whether another iteration is needed. In Reflexion, a human could review the episodic memory (accumulated reflections) to ensure the agent is learning useful rather than harmful heuristics. Both approaches are human-OFF-the-loop by default but can be adapted for human IN the loop at key decision points. The self-evaluation trace (the model's feedback/reflection) provides interpretability that enables human intervention — a human can read the model's self-critique and judge whether it is accurate before the next iteration.
override_capability: No built-in override mechanism. In principle: (a) the iterative loop can be interrupted at any point between iterations; (b) the output from any iteration can be replaced or modified by a human; (c) the feedback/reflection can be overridden — a human provides alternative feedback that guides the next iteration; (d) the episodic memory in Reflexion can be edited to remove harmful reflections. The self-evaluation trace makes override practical: a human can read the model's self-critique and say 'No, the problem is actually X, not Y — revise accordingly.' Override granularity is per-iteration (modify the output or feedback between iterations).
fallback_when_no_human: Fully autonomous operation. When no human is available, the self-improvement loop runs to completion (convergence or max iterations). If the self-evaluation is flawed and the converged output is still problematic, the system has no mechanism to detect this. The approaches provide no human escalation path — they will output whatever the final iteration produces, regardless of quality. For mission-critical applications, adding an external quality metric that triggers human escalation when the converged output falls below a threshold is essential — but this is not part of the published methods.
max_problem_size: Reflexion was demonstrated on: ALFWorld (household tasks, 5-15 action sequences), HumanEval (164 Python programming problems), HotpotQA (multi-hop QA). Reflexion achieved 91% pass@1 on HumanEval (GPT-4) vs. 80% for baseline GPT-4. Self-Refine was demonstrated on 7 diverse NLP tasks: dialogue response, code optimization, math reasoning, sentiment reversal, acronym generation, constrained text generation, and text detoxification. The theoretical limit is determined by: (a) the model's ability to provide meaningful self-feedback — as output complexity grows, self-evaluation becomes harder; (b) the number of iterations needed for convergence — complex outputs may require more iterations, increasing cost; (c) context window constraints — the full generation + feedback history must fit in the context window. What breaks at scale: (1) for very long outputs (thousands of tokens), self-feedback becomes shallow (the model cannot attend to all details); (2) for tasks requiring many iterations, the context window fills with earlier versions; (3) the model's self-evaluation degrades at scale (it cannot reliably judge quality of very long or complex outputs).
parallel_execution: No built-in parallel execution — both approaches are inherently sequential. Each iteration depends on the previous iteration's output and feedback. However, parallelism can be introduced: (a) multiple independent refinement chains can run in parallel for different aspects of a task; (b) multiple candidate outputs can be generated in parallel, evaluated, and the best selected (self-consistency combined with self-refine); (c) in Reflexion, multiple trial attempts can run in parallel with different reflection histories, and the best result is selected. These are extensions beyond the published methods. The sequential nature is a fundamental constraint: self-improvement is a serial process — you must evaluate before you can refine.
team_scaling_model: Not directly addressed — both approaches are single-model. Multi-agent extensions: (a) Cross-Refine — Agent A generates, Agent B provides feedback, Agent A refines. This separates the generator and evaluator roles across different agents with different system prompts or models, reducing the self-evaluation bias. (b) Multi-Agent Debate with Self-Refine — multiple agents generate and critique each other's outputs. (c) In Reflexion, different agents can maintain separate episodic memories, and the best-performing memory can be shared. Scaling with more agents: (a) multi-agent critique is generally more effective than self-critique because different agents have different blind spots; (b) cost scales linearly with the number of agents; (c) coordination overhead grows as agents must agree on or reconcile their critiques. The optimal configuration is typically 2-3 agents: a generator, a critic (different from generator), and optionally a second critic for consensus.
cost_scaling_model: Token cost scales linearly with the number of iterations: cost = O(I * (generate_cost + feedback_cost + refine_cost)). Each iteration involves: (a) generation/refinement output tokens; (b) feedback output tokens; (c) all prior context (previous outputs + feedback). The context grows with each iteration, so per-iteration cost increases over the loop: cost_per_iteration(i) = O(initial_prompt + sum of prior outputs and feedbacks). For I iterations: total cost = O(I * initial_prompt + I^2 * avg_output_size). In practice, with I=2-3 (diminishing returns), this quadratic growth is manageable. Compared to single-pass generation (I=1), Self-Refine is 2-4x more expensive but produces 20%+ quality improvement — a favorable cost-benefit ratio for quality-sensitive tasks. Reflexion's cost additionally includes environment interaction costs (function calls, code execution) for each trial. The branching factor is 1 (single refinement path). Cost is stable and predictable — the iteration cap provides a hard cost bound. Using cheaper models for FEEDBACK (GPT-3.5 for critique, GPT-4 for generation) can reduce cost without significantly hurting quality.
replanning_built_in: Self-improvement IS replanning — the entire approach IS a replanning mechanism. Every iteration is a replanning of the output based on self-critique. This is a first-class, designed replanning capability — replanning is not an afterthought but the core mechanism. In Reflexion, the reflection stored in episodic memory enables replanning across trials — later trials benefit from the accumulated reflections of earlier failures, which is a form of learning-to-replan. In Self-Refine, each refinement iteration is a local replanning of the output. The replanning scope is local (refine this specific output) rather than global (replan the entire task — though Reflexion's cross-trial reflection approximates global replanning by changing the overall approach). The approaches provide no formal replanning protocol — the replanning strategy emerges from the model's self-critique. This is both flexible (any aspect can be improved) and unreliable (the model may replan in the wrong direction).
trigger_mechanism: Self-Refine: the trigger to iterate is ALWAYS present — the loop runs for a fixed number of iterations or until convergence is detected. There is no conditional trigger — the assumption is that refinement always has potential to improve. The Self-Refine paper acknowledges this is suboptimal: for tasks where the model's initial output is already good, refinement can waste tokens or even degrade quality ('over-refinement'). Reflexion: the trigger to reflect is the completion of a trial with less-than-perfect results. If the trial succeeds, no reflection is needed and the loop terminates. If the trial fails, the failure triggers reflection. This is a conditional trigger based on environmental feedback — more efficient than always-refining. Additional triggers could include: (a) confidence threshold — reflect when the model's confidence in its output is low; (b) human intervention — a human triggers reflection when they identify a problem the model missed; (c) diversity check — trigger refinement when multiple generation attempts produce divergent outputs, indicating an ambiguous task.
adaptation_cost: The cost of one refinement iteration is the token cost of FEEDBACK + REFINE calls. For Self-Refine with 2 iterations on top of the initial generation: adaptation cost = ~100-200% of the initial generation cost (2 additional LLM calls, each processing the full context). For Reflexion: adaptation cost per trial = cost(ACT + OBSERVE + REFLECT). If the task requires 3 trials to succeed: total cost = 3 * trial_cost. Compared to single-pass approaches: Self-Refine costs 2-4x more for 20%+ quality improvement; this is generally favorable. State preservation: (a) Self-Refine preserves the full generation history (all versions + feedback), enabling the model to learn from earlier iterations; (b) Reflexion preserves reflections in episodic memory, enabling cross-trial learning. The adaptation cost is the price of quality — and the papers argue it's cost-effective compared to alternatives (fine-tuning, human feedback).
failure_recovery_mechanism: Self-improvement IS failure recovery — the approaches recover from poor initial outputs through iterative refinement. In Self-Refine: (1) Detection — the FEEDBACK step identifies problems in the current output; (2) Isolation — the FEEDBACK step specifies what aspects need fixing; (3) Recovery — the REFINE step addresses the identified problems; (4) Containment — the refinement preserves good aspects while fixing bad ones. The recovery mechanism is limited to problems the model can detect — if the model doesn't recognize a problem, it won't be fixed. In Reflexion: (1) Detection — the environment signals failure (test fails, task incomplete); (2) Isolation — the REFLECT step analyzes the specific causes of failure; (3) Recovery — the next trial applies the lessons from reflection; (4) Containment — reflections are stored per-trial, so a failed strategy is recorded and avoided in future trials. Reflexion's recovery is more robust than Self-Refine's because environmental feedback provides ground truth — the model cannot ignore a failed test. Self-Refine's recovery is weaker because it relies on the model's self-evaluation, which may be wrong.
output_artifacts: Self-Refine produces: (a) the initial output; (b) the feedback for each iteration (the model's self-critique); (c) the refined output for each iteration; (d) the final converged output. The feedback trace is a valuable artifact — it shows WHAT the model thought was wrong and HOW it attempted to fix it, providing interpretability and auditability. Reflexion produces: (a) the trial history — each attempt with its actions and observations; (b) the reflection for each failed trial — the model's analysis of what went wrong; (c) the episodic memory — the accumulated set of reflections across all trials; (d) the successful trial's output (or best attempt). The episodic memory is a unique artifact — it captures the agent's learning across trials and can be reused for similar future tasks (transfer learning). Both approaches produce richer artifacts than single-pass generation, providing visibility into the model's self-assessment process.
briefing_format: For Self-Refine: the briefing format is the refinement trace — initial output, feedback, refined output — showing the evolution of the output. For stakeholders, a summary: 'Initial output had issues A, B, C. After 2 refinement iterations, issues A and B were resolved. The final output scores X on quality metric Y.' The feedback trace provides natural-language rationale for changes. For Reflexion: the briefing format is the trial summary — attempt 1 failed due to X (reflection: 'I should do Y instead'), attempt 2 failed due to Z, attempt 3 succeeded. The episodic memory provides a compact summary of lessons learned. Both approaches produce human-readable traces that explain not just what the model did but why it changed its approach. This interpretability is a significant strength — stakeholders can understand and validate the model's self-improvement process.
traceability: Strong traceability for the improvement process: every change in the output can be traced to specific feedback, and every feedback can be traced to a specific iteration. In Self-Refine, the refinement trace shows: initial version -> feedback identifying issues -> refined version addressing those issues. This is an explicit, human-readable decision trail. In Reflexion, the trial history shows: action sequence -> environmental outcome -> reflection on what was wrong -> how the next trial was different. The episodic memory provides a traceable record of learned lessons. However, the model's internal reasoning about WHY it generated specific feedback (why it identified issue X but missed issue Y) is not captured — the feedback is the model's output, but the reasoning BEHIND the feedback is implicit. Traceability of the improvement rationale is strong; traceability of the feedback generation rationale is weaker.
agent_communication_protocol: Not applicable in the original single-agent papers. In multi-agent extensions: (a) the generator agent communicates its output to the critic agent via structured messages; (b) the critic agent communicates feedback back to the generator; (c) in Reflexion, the acting agent and reflecting agent could be separate, communicating through a shared episodic memory store. The communication pattern is producer-consumer with feedback: Agent A produces output, Agent B consumes and critiques it, Agent A receives the critique. Standard agent protocols (MCP, ACP, A2A) can carry the output and feedback messages. The communication is synchronous (Agent A waits for Agent B's critique before refining).
learning_memory_architecture: Reflexion has a first-class episodic memory architecture — this is one of its key contributions. The memory stores: (a) the trajectory of each trial (actions + observations); (b) the reflection for each failed trial (what went wrong, why, what to do differently); (c) the accumulated set of reflections across all trials. When starting a new trial, the agent retrieves relevant reflections from episodic memory to guide its behavior. This is a form of learning without weight updates — the agent improves through accumulated linguistic knowledge. Self-Refine has no persistent memory — each task is refined independently. However, the concepts are complementary: Self-Refine's feedback+refine loop can operate WITHIN a single Reflexion trial (refining the output before submitting to the environment), and Reflexion's episodic memory can store Self-Refine feedback patterns for reuse. Extensions: (a) Semantic Memory — store generalized lessons (abstract patterns) separate from specific episodes; (b) Procedural Memory — store refined strategies as reusable skills; (c) Cross-Task Transfer — apply reflections from one task type to similar tasks. JORDAN's Replan node is a primitive version of Reflexion — it replans based on execution feedback, but lacks the episodic memory and cross-trial learning that make Reflexion powerful.
latency_bounds: Not suitable for hard real-time — each iteration requires full LLM inference (seconds per iteration). Typical latency: Self-Refine with 2 iterations: 3 LLM calls (generate + feedback + refine + optional second feedback + second refine), ~10-30 seconds total for GPT-4. Reflexion with 3 trials: 3 * (action loop + reflection) LLM calls, ~30-90 seconds total, plus environment interaction time. Under time pressure: (a) reduce iterations (1 refinement instead of 2-3); (b) use faster models for feedback (GPT-3.5 instead of GPT-4); (c) parallel candidate generation + single refinement (generate 3 candidates, keep the best, refine it once). The approaches degrade gracefully — fewer iterations produce lower-quality but acceptable results. For interactive applications, 1-2 refinement iterations (5-10 seconds) is feasible; for batch applications, 3+ iterations may be worthwhile.
evaluation_benchmarking: Reflexion evaluated on: (1) HumanEval — coding benchmark. GPT-4 + Reflexion achieved 91% pass@1 (SOTA at the time) vs. 80% for baseline GPT-4. (2) ALFWorld — sequential decision-making benchmark. Reflexion significantly outperformed ReAct and BUTLER baselines. (3) HotpotQA — multi-hop QA. Reflexion improved answer accuracy over ReAct. Baselines: standard prompting, CoT, ReAct. Self-Refine evaluated on 7 diverse tasks: (1) dialogue response generation, (2) code optimization, (3) math reasoning, (4) sentiment reversal, (5) acronym generation, (6) constrained text generation, (7) text detoxification. Metrics: task-specific quality metrics + human preference evaluation. Self-Refine achieved ~20% average absolute improvement across tasks over direct generation. Model: GPT-3.5, ChatGPT, GPT-4. Key finding: Self-Refine works best with strong base models (GPT-4 > GPT-3.5). Both papers demonstrate that self-improvement loops produce meaningful, measurable quality improvements. Limitations: evaluation on relatively small, controlled tasks; no standardized agent benchmark comparison; human evaluation for some tasks (subjective quality).
mapping_strength: Strong mapping to JORDAN's Replan node and quality assurance needs. Reflexion's reflect-and-retry pattern maps directly to JORDAN's Replan node — when execution fails or produces low-quality output, the Replan node should: (a) analyze WHAT went wrong; (b) generate a verbal reflection on WHY; (c) store that reflection in mission memory; (d) produce a revised plan informed by the reflection. Self-Refine maps to JORDAN's quality assurance: before accepting any output (research findings, code, plan), JORDAN agents should self-critique and refine their outputs. The self-evaluation loop can be applied at multiple JORDAN nodes: (a) the Plan node refining its plan through self-critique; (b) the Research node refining its findings through iterative analysis; (c) worker agents refining their outputs before submitting to the orchestrator. What transfers directly: the self-reflection prompt pattern, the episodic memory for lesson accumulation, and the iterative refinement loop.
gaps: Key gaps for JORDAN: (1) Self-evaluation bias — the same model generating and evaluating its own output cannot reliably catch all errors, especially systematic blind spots. JORDAN needs cross-agent evaluation (Agent A critiques Agent B's output) rather than pure self-evaluation. (2) No ground-truth integration — Self-Refine relies purely on self-feedback without external verification; JORDAN needs to integrate test results, build outputs, and other hard feedback into the evaluation. (3) Episodic memory is trial-scoped — Reflexion's memory only spans trials of the same task; JORDAN needs cross-mission memory (lessons from Mission A informing Mission B). (4) No structured reflection format — reflections are free-text natural language; JORDAN needs structured reflections (what failed, why, recommended action) that can be automatically processed. (5) The reflexion cost adds to the already-significant JORDAN pipeline cost — each additional reflection or refinement is another LLM call. (6) Both approaches assume the initial quality gap is bridgeable through self-critique — for fundamentally wrong approaches, no amount of self-critique will fix the problem (the model must try a completely different strategy, which Reflexion supports via cross-trial learning but Self-Refine does not). (7) No integration with task decomposition — the approaches improve individual outputs but don't address the decomposition that produced the task assignments. (8) The 'self-critique paradox' — LLMs are generally worse at evaluating their own outputs than evaluating others' outputs, so Self-Refine's evaluation quality is inherently limited.
adaptation_required: For JORDAN: (1) Implement a structured reflection mechanism in the Replan node — when a mission phase fails or produces substandard results, the Replan node generates: (a) what specifically failed; (b) root cause analysis; (c) recommended strategy change; (d) confidence in the new strategy. This structured reflection replaces JORDAN's current implicit replanning. (2) Add episodic memory to JORDAN missions — store reflections from each mission phase in a mission-scoped memory that informs subsequent phases AND a cross-mission memory that informs future missions. (3) Implement cross-agent evaluation — before any output is accepted, a DIFFERENT agent (with a different system prompt or even a different model) critiques it. This addresses the self-evaluation bias. (4) Add Self-Refine loops to JORDAN's worker agents — each worker agent performs generate -> self-critique -> refine before submitting output to the orchestrator. The orchestrator receives pre-refined, higher-quality outputs. (5) Use Reflexion's cross-trial learning pattern for JORDAN's replanning — the Replan node should learn from failed replans within a mission (not just failed executions) to improve its replanning strategy over the course of a complex mission. (6) Implement reflection-based mission debriefing — after mission completion, generate a structured debrief that captures lessons learned, which is stored in cross-mission memory for future mission planning. (7) Add hard-feedback integration — combine self-evaluation with test results, build outputs, lint results, and other verifiable signals. The Replan node should weigh hard feedback more heavily than self-feedback. (8) Implement cost-efficient reflection — use a cheaper model for the REFLECTION/FEEDBACK step and a more capable model for the ACTION/REFINE step, reducing the cost of the improvement loop without sacrificing output quality.
validation_context: Reflexion published at NeurIPS 2023 (top-tier ML conference). Demonstrated SOTA performance on HumanEval (91% pass@1 with GPT-4). Self-Refine published at NeurIPS 2023 / ICLR 2024. Demonstrated consistent 20% average improvement across 7 diverse NLP tasks with GPT-3.5/4. Both papers have high citation counts as of 2025. Independently validated by: (a) the broader self-improvement literature (CRITIC, Self-Reflection, Cross-Refine, RISE); (b) integration into LangChain and other agent frameworks; (c) real-world deployment in code generation tools (GitHub Copilot's iterative refinement, though not explicitly using Self-Refine); (d) the emergence of self-critique as a standard LLM prompting technique. Limitations of validation: (a) both papers use controlled, relatively small-scale benchmarks — real-world generalization not established; (b) the HumanEval 91% result has been superseded by newer methods (though Reflexion's relative improvement remains significant); (c) the self-critique paradox (LLMs evaluate their own outputs less accurately than others' outputs) has been empirically validated in subsequent work, limiting Self-Refine's ceiling; (d) no evaluation on safety-critical tasks or multi-agent coordination.
known_failures: Documented failure modes: (1) Self-critique paradox — the model cannot detect errors it doesn't know are errors. If the model confidently believes a false statement, self-evaluation will not catch it. The Self-Refine paper acknowledges this limitation. (2) Over-refinement — the model changes aspects that were already correct, degrading output quality. This is particularly common in math reasoning where 'refining' introduces calculation errors. (3) Hallucinated feedback — the model identifies problems that don't exist ('the tone should be more professional' when the tone was already professional) and the refinement makes the output worse. (4) Feedback-is-too-vague — the model's feedback is generic ('improve the quality') rather than specific and actionable, leading to ineffective refinement. The quality of feedback is the critical factor — the Self-Refine paper shows that actionable, specific feedback drives improvement; vague feedback does not. (5) Convergence to suboptimal — the model refines toward a local optimum that is better than the initial output but still not good. This is particularly common when the initial approach was wrong and refinement only patches symptoms. (6) Reflexion: memory contamination — a reflection from one trial type is incorrectly applied to a different trial type ('When searching, I should always use Wikipedia' — applied even when Wikipedia is the wrong source). (7) Reflexion: reflection quality varies — the model reflects on surface-level issues (wrong search query) rather than structural issues (wrong overall strategy), leading to superficial improvement. (8) Iteration cost without benefit — the model iterates 5 times with no quality improvement (or degradation), wasting tokens. The Self-Refine paper shows that 2 iterations is the sweet spot for most tasks. (9) The approaches DON'T WORK with weak models — GPT-3.5-based Self-Refine shows minimal or negative improvement on some tasks because the model's self-evaluation is too poor to guide refinement. The method requires a capable base model. (10) Self-critique can amplify biases — if the model has a bias (e.g., toward formal language), self-critique may identify informality as a problem and refine toward even more formal language, amplifying the bias.
where_still_used: Both approaches are widely adopted in research and practice: (a) LangChain and LlamaIndex integrate self-reflection and self-refinement as standard agent capabilities; (b) the self-critique prompt pattern ('review your output and improve it') is now a standard LLM prompting technique used across the industry; (c) Reflexion's episodic memory concept has influenced memory architectures in AutoGPT, MemGPT, and other autonomous agent frameworks; (d) the concept of 'reflection' as a distinct agent step is standard in multi-agent frameworks (CrewAI, AutoGen); (e) code generation tools use iterative refinement loops (generate -> test -> self-critique -> regenerate) that parallel Reflexion's approach; (f) the papers' core ideas have been absorbed into the general knowledge of LLM application development — many developers use self-refinement without knowing the paper names. The specific Reflexion and Self-Refine implementations are less important than the PARADIGM they established: LLMs can improve their own outputs through structured self-evaluation and iterative refinement, and this improvement is substantial enough to justify the cost. JORDAN's current Replan node is a primitive version of this paradigm — implementing the full Reflexion/Self-Refine approach would significantly strengthen JORDAN's quality assurance and adaptive capability.
